tasks.prompt:
Warmup — Verify your tools are ready.

Before starting a cluster, confirm that Docker, your cluster tool (minikube or kind), and kubectl are all installed and accessible from your terminal. Run the three verification commands below and record what each one prints.
tasks.starter:
# Run each command and note the output.

# 1. Check Docker is running
docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}'

# 2. Check your cluster tool
minikube version          # if you chose minikube
# kind version            # if you chose kind

# 3. Check kubectl (client side only)
kubectl version --client
tasks.solution:
# Expected output (versions will differ on your machine):

# docker version
# 24.0.7

# minikube version
# minikube version: v1.33.1
# commit: 5883c09216182566a63dff4c326a6fc9ed2982ff

# kubectl version --client
# Client Version: v1.30.2
# Kustomize Version: v5.0.4-0.20230601165947-6ce0bf390ce3
tasks.explanation: These three commands confirm that the Docker daemon is responsive (a prerequisite for both minikube and kind), that your chosen cluster tool is on the PATH, and that kubectl is installed client-side. None of them touch a cluster yet — they simply verify the starting conditions. If any command fails, install or start the missing tool before proceeding.
hints: If `docker version` returns only the client section (no server), Docker Desktop is not running — start it and retry.
If `minikube` or `kind` is not found, follow the install instructions at minikube.sigs.k8s.io or kind.sigs.k8s.io and make sure the binary is in your PATH.
kubectl is usually installed as a dependency of minikube/kind. If it is still missing, install it separately from kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools.

tasks.prompt:
Core 1 — Start a local cluster and verify it is healthy.

Using minikube **or** kind, create a single-node Kubernetes cluster and confirm it is ready. You must see exactly one node with status `Ready` and a printed API-server URL before you move on.
tasks.starter:
# --- Option A: minikube ---
minikube start --driver=docker

# --- Option B: kind ---
kind create cluster --name capstone

# After the cluster is up, run these health checks (same for both):
kubectl get nodes
kubectl cluster-info
tasks.solution:
# minikube path
minikube start --driver=docker
# Wait for "Done! kubectl is now configured to use \"minikube\" cluster"

minikube status
# Expected:
# minikube
# type: Control Plane
# host: Running
# kubelet: Running
# apiserver: Running
# kubeconfig: Configured

kubectl get nodes
# NAME       STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
# minikube   Ready    control-plane   1m    v1.30.0

kubectl cluster-info
# Kubernetes control plane is running at https://127.0.0.1:<port>
# CoreDNS is running at https://127.0.0.1:<port>/api/v1/...

# -------------------------------------------------------
# kind path
kind create cluster --name capstone
# Wait for "Have a nice day!"

kubectl get nodes
# NAME                     STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
# capstone-control-plane   Ready    control-plane   1m    v1.30.0

kubectl cluster-info --context kind-capstone
# Kubernetes control plane is running at https://127.0.0.1:<port>
tasks.explanation: `minikube start --driver=docker` (or `kind create cluster`) does three things: it downloads a Kubernetes node image (once, cached thereafter), boots that node as a Docker container on your machine, and writes a new entry into `~/.kube/config` so kubectl knows how to reach the cluster. `kubectl get nodes` reads from the cluster's API server — if it returns a `Ready` node, kubectl is correctly wired up. `kubectl cluster-info` prints the API-server URL, confirming the control plane is reachable. Both checks together confirm the cluster is healthy before you invest time loading images.
hints: If `minikube start` reports 'Exiting due to PROVIDER_DOCKER_NOT_RUNNING', Docker Desktop is not running — start it first.

For kind, verify your kubectl context was switched automatically with `kubectl config current-context`; it should print `kind-capstone`. If not, run `kubectl cluster-info --context kind-capstone` explicitly.
A node stuck in `NotReady` usually means the cluster is still initialising. Wait 30-60 seconds and re-run `kubectl get nodes`.

tasks.prompt:
Core 2 — Load the `flask-capstone:1.0` image into the cluster.

The image you built in Lecture 2 lives in your local Docker daemon, but the cluster uses a separate internal image store and cannot see it yet. Copy the image into the cluster using the appropriate command for your tool, then verify it is present inside the cluster.
tasks.starter:
# Confirm the image exists in your local Docker first:
docker images flask-capstone
# Expected: a row showing flask-capstone  1.0  ...

# --- Option A: minikube ---
minikube image load flask-capstone:1.0
minikube image ls | grep flask-capstone

# --- Option B: kind ---
kind load docker-image flask-capstone:1.0 --name capstone
# Verification for kind (exec into the node):
docker exec -it capstone-control-plane crictl images | grep flask-capstone
tasks.solution:
# minikube path
minikube image load flask-capstone:1.0
# (no output on success; the command exits 0)

minikube image ls | grep flask-capstone
# docker.io/library/flask-capstone:1.0

# -------------------------------------------------------
# kind path
kind load docker-image flask-capstone:1.0 --name capstone
# Image: "flask-capstone:1.0" with ID "sha256:..." not yet present on node "capstone-control-plane", loading...

# Verify inside the node container:
docker exec -it capstone-control-plane crictl images | grep flask
# IMAGE                           TAG   IMAGE ID       SIZE
# docker.io/library/flask-capstone  1.0  sha256:...   ...
tasks.explanation: Both `minikube image load` and `kind load docker-image` copy the image tarball from your local Docker daemon across what the lecture calls the 'laptop-to-cluster boundary' — into the node container's own container runtime (containerd). After this step, the image lives inside the cluster and can be referenced in a Pod spec without pulling from any registry. The verification step proves the copy succeeded: `minikube image ls` lists images stored on the minikube node, and `crictl images` (run inside the kind node container) lists images known to containerd on that node.
hints: If `docker images flask-capstone` returns nothing, the image does not exist yet — go back to Lecture 2 and build it with `docker build -t flask-capstone:1.0 .`.
For minikube, a long pause after `minikube image load` is normal on first run; subsequent loads of the same image are instant because minikube caches it.
For kind, make sure you include `--name capstone` (or whatever name you used with `kind create cluster`). Omitting it targets the default cluster named `kind`, which may not exist.

tasks.prompt:
Applied — Understand `imagePullPolicy` and predict the failure.

Now that the image is loaded, answer the following two questions by checking the lecture content and then testing your understanding:

1. Write a minimal Pod spec YAML snippet that schedules the `flask-capstone:1.0` container with the correct `imagePullPolicy` for a local-only image. The Pod should not pull from any remote registry.

2. If you accidentally set `imagePullPolicy: Always` (or omitted the field on a `:latest`-tagged image), what error status would you see on the Pod, and why? Explain in 1-2 sentences.

Apply the Pod from question 1 to your running cluster and confirm it reaches `Running` status.
tasks.starter:
# Fill in the blank — write a Pod spec with the correct imagePullPolicy:
cat <<'EOF' > /tmp/flask-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: flask-test
spec:
  containers:
    - name: flask
      image: flask-capstone:1.0
      imagePullPolicy: _______________   # <-- fill this in
      ports:
        - containerPort: 5000
EOF

kubectl apply -f /tmp/flask-pod.yaml
kubectl get pod flask-test
tasks.solution:
# Question 1 — correct Pod spec:
cat <<'EOF' > /tmp/flask-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: flask-test
spec:
  containers:
    - name: flask
      image: flask-capstone:1.0
      imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
      ports:
        - containerPort: 5000
EOF

kubectl apply -f /tmp/flask-pod.yaml
# pod/flask-test created

kubectl get pod flask-test
# NAME         READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# flask-test   1/1     Running   0          10s

# ---
# Question 2 answer:
# If imagePullPolicy is Always (or the tag is :latest, which defaults to Always),
# Kubernetes will attempt to pull flask-capstone:1.0 from a remote registry.
# Because the image was never pushed, the pull fails and the Pod enters
# ImagePullBackOff — Kubernetes' signal that it cannot fetch the image.
tasks.explanation: `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent` tells the kubelet: 'if this image is already in the node's image store, use it; only reach out to a registry if it is absent.' Since we loaded `flask-capstone:1.0` in the previous task, it *is* present, so the kubelet uses it locally and the Pod starts without any network call to a registry. The pairing of a fixed, non-`:latest` tag with `IfNotPresent` is the standard recipe for local Kubernetes development. By contrast, `imagePullPolicy: Always` forces a fresh registry pull every time a Pod is scheduled — appropriate in CI/CD where you want the latest pushed image, but fatal here because no such registry entry exists.
hints: The lecture says to use `IfNotPresent` (not `Never`). Both work for a loaded image, but `IfNotPresent` is the conventional choice for local dev because it gracefully falls back to a registry pull if the image *is* available remotely.
If the Pod stays in `ContainerCreating` for more than a minute, describe it with `kubectl describe pod flask-test` and look at the Events section for clues.

To observe the `ImagePullBackOff` failure deliberately, create a second Pod with `imagePullPolicy: Always` and watch `kubectl get pod` — it will never reach `Running` because there is no remote registry entry.
